references.title: Early prediction of undergraduate Student's academic performance in completely online learning: A five-year study

extracted.Features: [Total_logins, N_access_forum, N_addedd_messages_forum, N_access_didactic_units, N_access_glossaries, Total_assignments, N_assignments_consulted, N_assignments_submited, N_access_questionnaires, N_attempts_questionnaires, N_answered_questions, N_questionnaire_views, N_questionnaires_submitted, N_reviews_questionnaire, Days_first_access, N_entries_course, Age, Sex, Degree, Grade Course, GPA_12_13, GPA_13_14, GPA 14_15, GPA_15_16, GPA_16_17]
extracted.Preprocessing Details:
All courses in UDIMA share a similar structure, which makes it easier to preprocess the data. All variables were measured at the end of the Academic Semester. GPA was computed (not provided by the system).

Variables were subjected to PCA to perform Exploratory Factor Analysis, which lead to the selection of 3 Factors.
extracted.Performance Metric: Best Model: {RSquared: 0.456 - 4 Variables - Course, Adjusted RSquared: 0.455 - 4 Variables - Course, RMSE: 0.126 - 4 Variables - Course}
extracted.Implications:
Short-term success is often determined by Task, Access, Questionnaire and Age. 

Factors that influence long-term sucess, however are the same (but without task).
extracted.Comments:
Authors perform regression both for success at the end of the course but also do it to predict the . Model performs much better at the end of program prediction.

Authors find that predicting success at the end of the course is significantly easier than  using the data from that course to predict success at the end of academic year. Moreover, the fits become gradually worse as the years pass. 

Models are course-agnostic because all courses considered share a similar structure.
extracted.Limitations:
Only use Linear Regression

No validation

No personal data was used - no prior knowledge was considered

Small sample size

extracted.Features: [Total_logins, N_access_forum, N_addedd_messages_forum, N_access_didactic_units, N_access_glossaries, Total_assignments, N_assignments_consulted, N_assignments_submited, N_access_questionnaires, N_attempts_questionnaires, N_answered_questions, N_questionnaire_views, N_questionnaires_submitted, N_reviews_questionnaire, Days_first_access, N_entries_course, Age, Sex, Degree, Grade Course, GPA_12_13, GPA_13_14, GPA 14_15, GPA_15_16, GPA_16_17]
extracted.Preprocessing Details:
All courses in UDIMA share a similar structure, which makes it easier to preprocess the data. All variables were measured at the end of the Academic Semester. GPA was computed (not provided by the system).

Variables were subjected to PCA to perform Exploratory Factor Analysis.
extracted.Cluster Description:
Group 1: Mainly CS, Journalism and Psychology - Younger and overall less engaged, which also translates in poorer performance.

Group 2: CS, Criminology and Psychology students. High proneness to questionnaires and assignments but still infrequent accesses. Course performance is high, but GPA is smaller than mean.

Group 3: Civil Engineering - Older students who tend to be more active in the course and achieve good short and long term results.

Group 4: Criminology, Journalism and Business Administration - Low participation in assignments but high participation in the remaining course materials.

Group 5: Law and Criminology - High levels of overall engagement,
extracted.Implications:
Older students seem to be more prone to have higher frequency of access to the different materials, suggesting a higher mastery of SRL behaviors.

Some students, who focus on practical tasks may get a good grade on the course but that performance does not seem to generalize as well to other courses → suggesting a more compartimentalized learning profile. 


Online institutions should focus on early intervention to help their students.
extracted.Comments: Clustering analysis using hierarchical clustering suggests groups that share multiple similarities with the Cerezo work → and, by extension… mine as well.

annotations.text: It is difficult to predict whether a specific student that is completely online is engaged or not. So, the study aims to use multiple methods to predict student performance.

Data used encompasses student carachteristics, Moodle interactions and grades. Results highlight that the main contributors to performance are: Access, Questionnaire, Task and Age. Clustering results also show that interactions are correlated with performance.
